exercises.title: GAIN STAGE
exercises.brief: Boost every signal by a factor of two.
promptLines: Define **doubleAll(nums: number[]): number[]** returning a NEW array with every number multiplied by `2`.
Reach for `map` — it transforms each element and hands back a fresh array.
starterLines: // TODO: map each number to itself times two
exercises.lore: Push every channel through the gain stage; the levels come back twice as hot.

exercises.title: NOISE GATE
exercises.brief: Drop what matches; keep the rest.
promptLines: Define **reject<T>(xs: T[], pred: (x: T) => boolean): T[]** returning the items for which `pred` is FALSE (the opposite of filter).
starterLines: function reject<T>(xs: T[], pred: (x: T) => boolean): T[] {
solutionLines: function reject<T>(xs: T[], pred: (x: T) => boolean): T[] {

exercises.title: RUN LENGTH
exercises.brief: Take the leading run that satisfies the test, then stop.
promptLines: Define **takeWhile<T>(xs: T[], pred: (x: T) => boolean): T[]** returning the longest PREFIX of `xs` whose elements all satisfy `pred`.
Stop at the FIRST element that fails — everything after it is dropped, even if it would pass.
starterLines: function takeWhile<T>(xs: T[], pred: (x: T) => boolean): T[] {
// TODO: collect from the front while pred holds, then break
solutionLines: function takeWhile<T>(xs: T[], pred: (x: T) => boolean): T[] {
exercises.hint: Loop from index 0; break on the first element where !pred(x), pushing the rest.
exercises.lore: Read the stream until the first bad packet, then cut — the run length is all you keep.

exercises.title: UNNEST GRID
exercises.brief: Collapse a list of lists down one level.
promptLines: Define a generic **flatten<T>(xss: T[][]): T[]** that concatenates every inner array into ONE flat array, in order.
Flatten exactly one level — fold the rows together with `reduce`.
starterLines: // TODO: reduce the rows into a single array via concat
solutionLines: return xss.reduce(function (acc: T[], xs: T[]) { return acc.concat(xs); }, [] as T[]);
exercises.lore: Drop the grid down one dimension — every row spills into the same line.

exercises.title: WEIGHTED TALLY
exercises.brief: Project each item to a number, then total them.
promptLines: Define a generic **sumOf<T>(xs: T[], f: (x: T) => number): number** that maps each element through `f` and returns the SUM of those numbers.
Use `reduce` with a starting accumulator of `0`, so an empty array returns `0`.
sumOf([{ n: 1 }, { n: 2 }, { n: 3 }], item => item.n) // 6
starterLines: function sumOf<T>(xs: T[], f: (x: T) => number): number {
// TODO: reduce, adding f(x) to a running total that starts at 0
solutionLines: function sumOf<T>(xs: T[], f: (x: T) => number): number {
return xs.reduce(function (acc, x) { return acc + f(x); }, 0);

tests.name: sums a projected field
tests.code: assertEqual(sumOf([{ n: 1 }, { n: 2 }, { n: 3 }], function (item) { return item.n; }), 6);

exercises.lore: Weigh each record, drop it on the scale, and read the running total.
